summary:
Ramesses II, who reigned from approximately 1279 to 1213 BC, was the third pharaoh of the Nineteenth Dynasty and the most celebrated ruler of the [[event:60|New Kingdom]] of Egypt. His reign of roughly 66 years was one of the longest in Egyptian history, and he outlived most of his children and several generations of officials and priests. Known to later tradition as Ramesses the Great, he pursued an aggressive construction program, a vast family (reportedly fathering over ninety children), and a foreign policy of both military confrontation and sophisticated diplomacy.

The defining military event of Ramesses II's reign was the [[event:1808|Battle of Kadesh]] in 1274 BC, fought against the Hittites under King Muwatalli II near the Orontes River in Syria. The battle was a near-catastrophic engagement in which the Egyptian army was ambushed and Ramesses II fought to avoid complete encirclement — an episode he celebrated in heroic reliefs and inscriptions plastered across temples throughout Egypt. Despite the inconclusive military result, the battle eventually led to the [[event:1809|Egyptian-Hittite Peace Treaty]] of 1259 BC, the earliest surviving international peace agreement in history.

Ramesses II's building program was unmatched in scope. He constructed, enlarged, or claimed credit for temples throughout Egypt and Nubia, including the colossal [[event:1810|Abu Simbel]] complex carved from living rock in Nubia, the Ramesseum mortuary temple at Thebes, extensive additions to the [[event:1885|Karnak Temple]], and the vast city of Pi-Ramesses in the Delta. His practice of inscribing his name over monuments of earlier kings means that Ramesses II is the most attested pharaoh in the archaeological record.

The latter decades of Ramesses II's reign coincided with growing external pressures on Egyptian power, including increasing pressure from the [[event:1814|Sea Peoples]] and internal economic strains from the cost of his massive building program. Nevertheless, his reign is remembered as the last great flowering of Egyptian imperial power before the turbulent collapse of the late Bronze Age. Ramesses II is commonly identified — though without conclusive proof — as the pharaoh of the biblical Exodus narrative.

The mummy of Ramesses II, discovered in the Deir el-Bahri royal cache in 1881, is among the best preserved of all royal mummies and has been extensively studied using modern scientific techniques. A detailed examination in the 1970s revealed that he suffered from arthritis, dental abscesses, and arteriosclerosis in his later years, consistent with his great age. He remains one of the most recognizable figures of the ancient world.
